text: Hi, I'm Glen. I'm going to talk to you about Oak Log, which is a cooperative experiment
that we've started in the Oakland area to build a community calendar, so a kind of commons
for event data. And for a little context for approach, I have some scar tissue from crypto,
and so one of the things that I really like this term sewer socialism, and I think Zoran
in New York is one of the folks that exemplifies this, which is you win someone's trust over
through an outcome rather than ideology. So I think as we've gone through this project,
even though I think about it as like digital public media infrastructure, I try to really
ground it in we are helping Oaklanders and other Bay Area folk find and go to local events,
and we happen to be using that protocol. This all starts with the Oakland Review of Books,
which is a new alt weekly that helped start last year. And as part of the Oakland Review
of books, we started to publish a community calendar of literary events, of cultural events,
of protests, and it got some real great pickup. And so one of the things I saw, this is Martina,
who does a lot of this work for us, and it was a huge pain for her to do this work. All of these
events are fragmented all over the place, so getting all that data in one place was a real pain. And then
the part that she actually really liked to do that was also really valuable for our audience,
which is that curation, the taste, telling people why that this might be an interesting thing to go to.
And that was a tiny portion of the work that she actually got to do. So we built some tools,
Discord bot and some other CMS tools just to help us manage this. And one of the things we started
realizing as we talked to lots of other people around the Bay is that there's there's tons,
there's dozens of folks that are doing the same thing, the same putting together these kind of curated
calendars. And so what we decided to do is just get them all in one room. So we invited all of the folks,
the public media station, the neighborhood calendar makers, there's lots of folks, alt weeklies,
newsrooms, all that are doing the same thing of managing calendars and helping people find events. So we got
everybody together and we talked about how might we share practices, share tools, share data, what would that
look like? And one thing that came out of that is everyone had the same sense of how do we do this thing
together? We need a mega calendar, but but not dystopian. And so we can just do things. And so we started to talk
about what it might look like to do this together on an open protocol. And so we kind of walked through and I think
those are some of the lessons of how we've talked about this is, so you know, starting with something like on
Instagram reels, where you have a venue publishing their data in this form, we've moved that out now into a database that each of our individual
organizations are using, whether it's Grist or Airtable or Excel, what have you. And the next step is, can we do this in a
shared database? Then, you know, here's the at proto record of a calendar event. And so, you know, one of the helpful
points here is, you know, can you start wherever you get your events, much like wherever you get to your podcasts, and
actually have that event data that venues want out there anyways, actually be available. And then there's a lot more we
can start doing with it. And some of the advantages that were really attractive to the publishers is one is how do we
start sharing these workflow tools. So we have our discord bot, there are others who have been building tools that are
scraping venue sites or big event sites, and tons of folks where it's just how does the venue actually create their own
event or submit it to publishers, all going into the same lexicon, the same data that we can then use.
And on the other end, we start being able to do a lot more with that data now that we've all
collaborated on that shared element. So one is we can still maintain editorial control, we can still
curate what is showing up in our individual publications and the commentary that we put around
it, but we can also collaborate. So if we add an event, then Coyote, you know, if Orb adds something,
and Coyote, your same page SF can use that same, that same data, we can play with whatever format we like,
whether it's in a post or in a calendar, and really excited to start exploring more of the social
functionality of can we can we see what folks in your social network are interested in attending or
going and not have it be specific to Luma or Eventbrite or others, I think it starts getting much more
attractive from the user perspective. So focusing a little bit more on the publisher control piece,
so we can still publish as we do in a newsletter. But I think there's some other interesting examples
here. This middle one is from Roundabout at New Public. And one of the things that they're really
focused on is how do you moderate, you know, have a local neighborhood steward actually be able to
moderate what goes on there. I think they're using the same, same schema that we are, I think there's
going to be some really interesting ways to interoperate there. Another really interesting example,
this is a group down in the Bay called the relational tech project. And what they're doing is enabling
neighborhood stewards to use AI tools to build their own applications, including things like
calendar apps. And so and allow that to be much more situated and kind of fit for what that community
needs. So, you know, the community of the outer sunset, for instance, in San Francisco also wants to see,
you know, how the waves are, for instance, and incorporate things that make sense for that community.
So what's next from here is after our community calendar conference, we had not only a bunch of
these media organizations excited, we have a bunch of people who really want to collaborate on the tech
side. And one in particular, it's really interesting is KQED, which is our big local NPR station that is
really interested in anchoring and supporting this. And so we're starting to go down the path of what might
it look like to create a community PDS for the Oakland Bay Area and specifically starting with with
events. We're also starting to explore new local applications that actually take advantage of this
collaboration. So one organization is thinking about doing a pop up calendar that's kind of poorly suited for
existing things like Yelp and others. So that's one thing we're looking into. Others are things more like a TV
guide-ish, like what's happening right now in Oakland, the Bay Area. So I think there's going to be a lot of
things that we can start building on top of this. We're starting with the media curators that are
already trusted, that already have audience, that already have attention. But from there, we're really excited
to expand that participation and get more venues involved and ultimately more residents. And then we're also
looking at beyond what this group can do together just on events, but thinking more generally about what
does the local community API look like? What is all the public data that we could could share? I think
there's lots of other use cases of things like buy nothing groups or lending libraries or, you know,
lots of things that exist on kind of the margins of existing social platforms that if we had more of a
public spectrum for the open social web, we can see really different applications develop. Much like in the US,
the kind of content you see on PBS or NPR is quite a bit different than the way you see commercially.
And then ideally, this all feeds into, hey, if this works in Oakland in the Bay Area and works for this
NPR station or this Alt Weekly, how do we create this playbook that allows other geographies to do
similar things of building for themselves? Yeah, so that's where we are. It's all kind of emergent,
but I think what I really love about it is it feels like a great demonstration of what I think is the
promise of these applications on that protocol, which is really by collaborating across organization,
can we actually build something that has a better experience for the users? You don't need to know
anything about how this is built other than everybody has collaborated to make it better,
and that's what you see. And so we can just do things together is the point. So let's hope on.
